text: Zone 2 training (low-intensity exercise below lactate threshold) is not supported by current evidence as the optimal intensity for improving mitochondrial or fatty acid oxidative capacity in the general population.

The association between Zone 2 training and high mitochondrial capacity is largely based on observational data from elite endurance athletes who perform large training volumes, and should not be generalized to the general public.
Higher exercise intensities (above Zone 2) are critical to maximize cardiometabolic health benefits, particularly when training volume is low.
Substantial evidence supports high-intensity exercise over Zone 2 training for improving mitochondrial capacity and cardiometabolic health in the general population.

The benefit of Zone 2 training for mitochondrial adaptations may be volume-dependent, requiring the large training volumes typical of elite athletes rather than the lower volumes common in the general population.
